Initially assumed some [jurisdiction](/wiki/Jurisdiction "Jurisdiction") from:  * [Parliament of the Province of Canada](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_Province_of_Canada "Parliament of the Province of Canada") * [General Assembly of Nova Scotia](/wiki/General_Assembly_of_Nova_Scotia "General Assembly of Nova Scotia") * [New Brunswick Legislature](/wiki/New_Brunswick_Legislature "New Brunswick Legislature")   Later added some jurisdiction from:   * [Hudson's Bay Company](/wiki/Hudson%27s_Bay_Company "Hudson's Bay Company") * [Legislative Council of British Columbia](/wiki/Legislative_Council_of_British_Columbia "Legislative Council of British Columbia") * [General Assembly of Prince Edward Island](/wiki/General_Assembly_of_Prince_Edward_Island "General Assembly of Prince Edward Island") * [General Assembly of Newfoundland](/wiki/General_Assembly_of_Newfoundland "General Assembly of Newfoundland") ([then suspended](/wiki/Commission_of_Government "Commission of Government")) * [Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom")

([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Parlement du Canada*) is the [federal](/wiki/Canadian_federalism "Canadian federalism") legislature of [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"). The [Crown](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Canada "Monarchy of Canada"), along with two chambers: the [Senate](/wiki/Senate_of_Canada "Senate of Canada") and the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_Canada "House of Commons of Canada"), form the [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameralism "Bicameralism") legislature.

The **Parliament of Canada** ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Parlement du Canada*) is the [federal](/wiki/Canadian_federalism "Canadian federalism") legislature of [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"). The [Crown](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Canada "Monarchy of Canada"), along with two chambers: the [Senate](/wiki/Senate_of_Canada "Senate of Canada") and the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_Canada "House of Commons of Canada"), form the [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameralism "Bicameralism") legislature.

The 343 members of the [lower house](/wiki/Lower_house "Lower house"), the House of Commons, are styled as [*Members of Parliament*](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(Canada) "Member of Parliament (Canada)") (MPs), and each elected to represent an [electoral district](/wiki/Electoral_district_(Canada) "Electoral district (Canada)") (also known as a riding). The 105 members of the [upper house](/wiki/Upper_house "Upper house"), the Senate, are styled *senators* and appointed by the [governor general](/wiki/Governor_General_of_Canada "Governor General of Canada") on the advice of the [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Canada "Prime Minister of Canada"). Collectively, MPs and senators are known as *parliamentarians*.

Bills may originate in either the House of Commons or the Senate, however, bills involving raising or spending funds must originate in the House of Commons. By [constitutional convention](/wiki/Constitutional_convention_(political_custom) "Constitutional convention (political custom)"), and following the [Westminster system](/wiki/Westminster_system "Westminster system") of government, the House of Commons is dominant, with the Senate rarely opposing its will. The Crown provides [royal assent](/wiki/Royal_assent "Royal assent") to make bills into law. The federal fiscal year runs from April 1 to March 31.[[1]](#cite_note-gm2-1)

The governor general, on the advice of the prime minister, summons and calls together the House of Commons, and may [prorogue](/wiki/Prorogation_in_Canada "Prorogation in Canada") or [dissolve Parliament](/wiki/Dissolution_of_parliament#Canada "Dissolution of parliament"), in order to either end a [parliamentary session](/wiki/Parliamentary_session "Parliamentary session") or [call a general election](/wiki/Dropping_the_writ "Dropping the writ"). The governor general also delivers the [throne speech](/wiki/Speech_from_the_throne "Speech from the throne") at the opening of each new Parliament (the monarch occasionally has done so instead of the governor general).

The election for the [45th Canadian Parliament](/wiki/45th_Canadian_Parliament "45th Canadian Parliament") was held on 28 April 2025, resulting in a Liberal minority government.

## Composition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Parliament_of_Canada&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Composition")]

The body consists of the [King of Canada](/wiki/King_of_Canada "King of Canada"), represented by a [viceroy](/wiki/Viceroy "Viceroy"), the [governor general](/wiki/Governor_General_of_Canada "Governor General of Canada"); an [upper house](/wiki/Upper_house "Upper house"), the [Senate](/wiki/Senate_of_Canada "Senate of Canada"); and a [lower house](/wiki/Lower_house "Lower house"), the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_Canada "House of Commons of Canada"). Each element has its own officers and organization. Each has a distinct role, but work in conjunction within the [legislative process](/wiki/Parliamentary_procedure "Parliamentary procedure"). This format was inherited from the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") and is a near-identical copy of the [Parliament at Westminster](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom"), the greatest differences stemming from situations unique to Canada, such as the impermanent nature of the monarch's residency in the country and the lack of a [peerage](/wiki/Peerage "Peerage") to form the upper chamber.

Only those who sit in the House of Commons are usually called [members of Parliament](/wiki/Members_of_Parliament#Canada "Members of Parliament") (MPs); the term is not usually applied to senators (except in legislation, such as the *[Parliament of Canada Act](/wiki/Parliament_of_Canada_Act "Parliament of Canada Act")*), even though the Senate is a part of Parliament. Though legislatively less powerful, senators take higher positions in the [national order of precedence](/wiki/Canadian_order_of_precedence "Canadian order of precedence"). No individual may serve in more than one chamber at the same time.

### Monarch

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Parliament_of_Canada&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Monarch")]

Main article: [Monarchy of Canada § Parliament (King-in-Parliament)](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Canada#Parliament_(King-in-Parliament) "Monarchy of Canada")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/2/23/Canada%27s_King_addresses_Parliament.jpg/250px-Canada%27s_King_addresses_Parliament.jpg)](/wiki/File:Canada%27s_King_addresses_Parliament.jpg)

[Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III"), [King of Canada](/wiki/King_of_Canada "King of Canada")

The sovereign's place in the legislature, formally known as the [King-in-Parliament](/wiki/King-in-Parliament "King-in-Parliament"),[[2]](#cite_note-2) is defined by the *[Constitution Act, 1867](/wiki/Constitution_Act,_1867 "Constitution Act, 1867")*, and various [conventions](/wiki/Convention_(norm)#Government "Convention (norm)").[[3]](#cite_note-CA1867-17-3) Neither he nor his viceroy, however, participates in the legislative process save for signifying the King's approval to a bill passed by both houses of Parliament, known as [royal assent](/wiki/Royal_assent "Royal assent"), which is necessary for a bill to be enacted as law. All federal bills thus begin with the phrase "Now, therefore, His Majesty, by and with the advice and consent of the Senate and House of Commons of Canada, enacts as follows ..."[[4]](#cite_note-4) and, as such, [the Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown") is immune from acts of Parliament unless expressed otherwise in the act itself.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The governor general normally grants royal assent, though the monarch may also do so, at the request of either the [Cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Canada "Cabinet of Canada") or the viceroy, who may defer assent to the sovereign as per the constitution.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

As both the monarch and his or her representatives are traditionally barred from the House of Commons (because [Charles I](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England") tried to personally arrest [five members](/wiki/Five_Members "Five Members") in the English Commons chamber in 1642), any parliamentary ceremonies in which they are involved take place in the Senate chamber. The upper and lower houses do, however, each contain a [mace](/wiki/Ceremonial_mace#Canada "Ceremonial mace"), which indicates the authority of the King-in-Parliament and the privilege granted to that body by him,[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) both bearing a crown at their apex. The original mace for the Senate was that used in the [Legislative Council of the Province of Canada](/wiki/Legislative_Council_of_the_Province_of_Canada "Legislative Council of the Province of Canada") after 1849, while that of the House of Commons was inherited from the [Legislative Assembly of the Province of Canada](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_the_Province_of_Canada "Legislative Assembly of the Province of Canada"), first used in 1845. Following the [burning of the Centre Block](/wiki/Centre_Block#Great_fire "Centre Block") on 3 February 1916, the [City of London](/wiki/City_of_London "City of London"), England, donated a replacement, which is still used today. The temporary mace, made of wood, and used until the new one arrived from the United Kingdom in 1917, is still carried into the Senate each 3 February.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The Senate's 1.6-metre-long mace comprises brass and gold. The Senate may not sit if its mace is not in the chamber; it typically sits on the table with the crown facing the throne,[[10]](#cite_note-SenatePP-10): 55 though it may, during certain ceremonies, be held by the mace bearer, standing adjacent to the governor general or monarch in the Senate.[[10]](#cite_note-SenatePP-10): 51

Members of the two houses of Parliament must also express their loyalty to the sovereign and defer to his authority, as the [Oath of Allegiance](/wiki/Oath_of_Allegiance_(Canada) "Oath of Allegiance (Canada)") must be sworn by all new parliamentarians before they may take their seats. Further, the [official opposition](/wiki/Official_Opposition_(Canada) "Official Opposition (Canada)") is formally called [His Majesty's Loyal Opposition](/wiki/Loyal_opposition "Loyal opposition"), to signify that, though they may be opposed to the incumbent Cabinet's policies, they remain dedicated to the apolitical Crown.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

### Senate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Parliament_of_Canada&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Senate")]

Main article: [Senate of Canada](/wiki/Senate_of_Canada "Senate of Canada")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Cansenate.jpg/250px-Cansenate.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cansenate.jpg)

The [Senate](/wiki/Senate_of_Canada "Senate of Canada")

The [upper house](/wiki/Upper_house "Upper house") of the Parliament of Canada, the Senate ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): Sénat), is a group of 105 individuals appointed by the governor general on the advice of the prime minister;[[13]](#cite_note-13) all those appointed must, per the constitution, be a minimum of 30 years old, be a subject of the monarch, and own property with a net worth of at least $4,000, in addition to owning land worth no less than $4,000 within the province the candidate seeks to represent.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Senators served for life until 1965, when a constitutional amendment imposed a mandatory retirement age of 75. Senators may, however, resign their seats prior to that mark, and can lose their position should they fail to attend two consecutive sessions of Parliament.

The principle underlying the Senate's composition is equality amongst Canada's geographic regions (called Divisions in the Constitution): 24 for [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario"), 24 for [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec"), 24 for the [Maritimes](/wiki/Maritimes "Maritimes") (10 for [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia"), 10 for [New Brunswick](/wiki/New_Brunswick "New Brunswick"), and four for [Prince Edward Island](/wiki/Prince_Edward_Island "Prince Edward Island")), and 24 for the [Western provinces](/wiki/Western_Canada "Western Canada") (six each for [Manitoba](/wiki/Manitoba "Manitoba"), [British Columbia](/wiki/British_Columbia "British Columbia"), [Saskatchewan](/wiki/Saskatchewan "Saskatchewan"), and [Alberta](/wiki/Alberta "Alberta")).[[15]](#cite_note-15) Additionally, senators are appointed from two geographic areas not part of any senatorial division. [Newfoundland and Labrador](/wiki/Newfoundland_and_Labrador "Newfoundland and Labrador") (since 1949 the "newest" province, although ["oldest" English settlement](/wiki/St._John%27s,_Newfoundland_and_Labrador#The_oldest_European_settlement_in_North_America_controversy "St. John's, Newfoundland and Labrador")), is represented by six senators. Since 1975 each of Canada's territories is represented by 1 senator—the [Northwest Territories](/wiki/Northwest_Territories "Northwest Territories"), [Yukon](/wiki/Yukon "Yukon"), and (since its formation in 1999) [Nunavut](/wiki/Nunavut "Nunavut"). An additional 4 or 8 senators may be appointed by the governor general, provided the approval of the King is secured and the four divisions are equally represented. This power has been employed once since 1867: to ensure the passage of the bill establishing the [Goods and Services Tax](/wiki/Goods_and_Services_Tax_(Canada) "Goods and Services Tax (Canada)"), Prime Minister [Brian Mulroney](/wiki/Brian_Mulroney "Brian Mulroney") advised Queen Elizabeth II to appoint extra senators in 1990. This results in a temporary maximum number of senators of 113, which must through attrition return to its normal number of 105.

### House of Commons

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Parliament_of_Canada&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: House of Commons")]

Main article: [House of Commons of Canada](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_Canada "House of Commons of Canada")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Ottawa_-_Parliament_Hill_-_Commons.jpg/250px-Ottawa_-_Parliament_Hill_-_Commons.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ottawa_-_Parliament_Hill_-_Commons.jpg)

The [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_Canada "House of Commons of Canada")

The elected component of the Canadian Parliament is the House of Commons ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): Chambre des communes), with each member chosen by a plurality of voters in each of the country's federal [electoral districts](/wiki/Electoral_district_(Canada) "Electoral district (Canada)"), or ridings. To run for one of the 343[[16]](#cite_note-seats-16) seats in the House of Commons, an individual must be a Canadian citizen and at least 18 years old. Each member holds office until Parliament is dissolved, after which they may seek re-election. The ridings are regularly reorganized according to the results of each decennial national [census](/wiki/Census "Census");[[17]](#cite_note-ElizabethIII.2-17) however, the "senatorial clause" of the *Constitution Act, 1867* guarantees each province at least as many MPs as it has senators,[[18]](#cite_note-18) and the "grandfather clause" permits each province as many MPs as it had in either 1976 or 1985.[[17]](#cite_note-ElizabethIII.2-17) The existence of this legislation has pushed the size of the House of Commons above the required minimum of 282 seats.

## Jurisdiction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Parliament_of_Canada&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Jurisdiction")]

The powers of the Parliament of Canada are limited by the constitution, which divides legislative abilities between the federal and [provincial legislatures](/wiki/Provincial_Legislatures_of_Canada "Provincial Legislatures of Canada"); in general, provincial [legislatures](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly "Legislative Assembly") may only pass laws relating to topics explicitly reserved for them by the constitution (such as education, provincial officers, municipal government, charitable institutions, and "matters of a merely local or private nature")[[19]](#cite_note-19) while any matter not under the exclusive authority of the provincial legislatures is within the scope of the federal Parliament's power. Thus, Parliament alone can pass laws relating to, among other things, the postal service, census, [military](/wiki/Canadian_Forces "Canadian Forces"), navigation and shipping, fishing, currency, banking, weights and measures, bankruptcy, copyrights, patents, [First Nations](/wiki/Aboriginal_people_in_Canada "Aboriginal people in Canada"), and [naturalization](/wiki/Naturalization "Naturalization").[[20]](#cite_note-20) In some cases, however, the jurisdictions of the federal and provincial parliaments may be more vague. For instance, the federal parliament regulates marriage and divorce in general, but the solemnization of marriage is regulated only by the provincial legislatures. Other examples include the powers of both the federal and provincial parliaments to impose taxes, borrow money, punish crimes, and regulate agriculture.

The powers of Parliament are also limited by the *[Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms](/wiki/Canadian_Charter_of_Rights_and_Freedoms "Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms")*, though most of its provisions can be overridden by use of the [notwithstanding clause](/wiki/Section_Thirty-three_of_the_Canadian_Charter_of_Rights_and_Freedoms "Section Thirty-three of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms").[[21]](#cite_note-21) Such clause, however, has never been used by the federal parliament, though it has been employed by some provincial legislatures. Laws violating any part of the constitution are invalid and may be ruled unconstitutional by [the courts](/wiki/Canadian_court_system "Canadian court system").

## Officers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Parliament_of_Canada&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Officers")]

Each of Parliament's two chambers is presided over by a [speaker](/wiki/Speaker_(politics) "Speaker (politics)"); that for the Senate is a member appointed by the governor general on the advice of the prime minister, while the [equivalent for the House of Commons](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_House_of_Commons_(Canada) "Speaker of the House of Commons (Canada)") is a member of Parliament, who is elected by the other members of that body. In general, the powers of the latter are greater than those of the former. Following the British model, the upper chamber is essentially self-regulating, but the lower chamber is controlled by the chair, in a majoritarian model that gives great power and authority to the chair. In 1991, however, the powers of the [speaker of the Senate](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_Senate_of_Canada "Speaker of the Senate of Canada") were expanded, which reorganized the balance of power to be closer to the framework of the Commons.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Kevin_MacLeod_in_Canadian_Senate_Chamber_2009.jpg/250px-Kevin_MacLeod_in_Canadian_Senate_Chamber_2009.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kevin_MacLeod_in_Canadian_Senate_Chamber_2009.jpg)

[Usher of the black rod](/wiki/Usher_of_the_Black_Rod_(Canada) "Usher of the Black Rod (Canada)"), [Kevin S. MacLeod](/wiki/Kevin_S._MacLeod "Kevin S. MacLeod"), before the thrones in the Senate chamber, 2009

The [usher of the black rod of the Senate of Canada](/wiki/Usher_of_the_Black_Rod_(Canada) "Usher of the Black Rod (Canada)") is the most senior protocol position in Parliament, being the personal messenger to the legislature of the sovereign and governor general. The usher is also a floor officer of the Senate responsible for security in that chamber, as well as for protocol, administrative, and logistical details of important events taking place on Parliament Hill,[[22]](#cite_note-22) such as the [Speech from the Throne](/wiki/Speech_from_the_Throne "Speech from the Throne"), Royal Assent ceremonies, [state funerals](/wiki/State_funeral "State funeral"), or the investiture of a new governor general.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

Other officers of Parliament include the [auditor general](/wiki/Auditor_General_of_Canada "Auditor General of Canada"), [chief electoral officer](/wiki/Chief_Electoral_Officer_(Canada) "Chief Electoral Officer (Canada)"), [official languages commissioner](/wiki/Official_Languages_Commissioner "Official Languages Commissioner"), [privacy commissioner](/wiki/Privacy_Commissioner_of_Canada "Privacy Commissioner of Canada"), [information commissioner](/wiki/Information_Commissioner_of_Canada "Information Commissioner of Canada"), [conflict of interest and ethics commissioner](/wiki/Conflict_of_Interest_and_Ethics_Commissioner "Conflict of Interest and Ethics Commissioner"), [public sector integrity commissioner](/wiki/Public_Sector_Integrity_Commissioner "Public Sector Integrity Commissioner"), and [commissioner of lobbying](/wiki/Commissioner_of_Lobbying "Commissioner of Lobbying"). These individuals are appointed by either one or both houses, to which they report through the speaker of that house. They are sometimes referred to as *Agents of Parliament*.[[24]](#cite_note-24) Another key official is the [parliamentary librarian](/wiki/Parliamentary_Librarian_of_Canada "Parliamentary Librarian of Canada"), a position established in 1871 under the *Library of Parliament Act*, charged with directing the [Library of Parliament](/wiki/Library_of_Parliament "Library of Parliament").

## Term

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Parliament_of_Canada&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Term")]

The *Constitution Act, 1867*, outlines that the governor general alone is responsible for summoning Parliament, though it remains the monarch's [prerogative](/wiki/Royal_prerogative "Royal prerogative") to [prorogue](/wiki/Prorogation_in_Canada "Prorogation in Canada") and [dissolve](/wiki/Dissolution_of_Parliament "Dissolution of Parliament") the legislature, after which the [writs](/wiki/Writ_of_election "Writ of election") for a [general federal election](/wiki/Elections_in_Canada#National_elections "Elections in Canada") are usually [dropped by the governor general](/wiki/Dropping_the_writ#Canada "Dropping the writ") at [Rideau Hall](/wiki/Rideau_Hall "Rideau Hall"). Upon completion of the election, the governor general, on the advice of the prime minister, then issues a royal [proclamation](/wiki/Proclamation "Proclamation") summoning Parliament to assemble. On the date given, new MPs are sworn in and then are, along with returning MPs, called to the Senate, where they are instructed to elect their speaker and return to the House of Commons to do so before adjourning.[[10]](#cite_note-SenatePP-10): 42

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Queen_Elizabeth_II_and_Prince_Philip_sit_on_thrones_before_a_full_Parliament.jpg/250px-Queen_Elizabeth_II_and_Prince_Philip_sit_on_thrones_before_a_full_Parliament.jpg)](/wiki/File:Queen_Elizabeth_II_and_Prince_Philip_sit_on_thrones_before_a_full_Parliament.jpg)

Queen Elizabeth II and Prince Philip at the opening of Parliament, 14 October 1957

The new parliamentary session is marked by the [opening of Parliament](/wiki/Opening_of_the_Canadian_parliament "Opening of the Canadian parliament"), a ceremony where a range of topics can be addressed in a [Speech From the Throne](/wiki/Speech_from_the_throne "Speech from the throne") given by the monarch, the governor general, or a royal delegate.[[note 1]](#cite_note-Open-25) The usher of the black rod invites MPs to these events,[[25]](#cite_note-26) knocking on the doors of the lower house that have been slammed shut[[26]](#cite_note-27)—a symbolic arrangement designed to illustrate the Commons' right to deny entry to anyone, including even the monarch (but with an exception for royal messengers).[[27]](#cite_note-28) Once the MPs are gathered behind the Bar of the Senate—save for the prime minister, the only MP permitted into the Senate proper to sit near the throne dais—the House of Commons speaker presents to the monarch or governor general, and formally claims the rights and privileges of the House of Commons; and then the speaker of the Senate, on behalf of the Crown, replies in acknowledgement after the sovereign or viceroy takes their seat on the throne.[[10]](#cite_note-SenatePP-10): 42 The speech is then read aloud. It can outline the program of the [Cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Canada "Cabinet of Canada") for the upcoming legislative session, as well as other matters chosen by the speaker.

A [parliamentary session](/wiki/Legislative_session "Legislative session") lasts until a prorogation, after which, without ceremony, both chambers of the legislature cease all legislative business until the governor general issues another proclamation calling for a new session to begin; except for the election of a speaker for the House of Commons and his or her claiming of that house's privileges, the same procedures for the opening of Parliament are again followed. After a number of such sessions—having ranged from one to seven[[10]](#cite_note-SenatePP-10): 45—a Parliament comes to an end via [dissolution](/wiki/Dissolution_of_parliament "Dissolution of parliament"), and a general election typically follows. Subject to the governor general's discretion, general elections are held four years after the previous on the third Monday in October or, on the recommendation of the [chief electoral officer](/wiki/Chief_Electoral_Officer_(Canada) "Chief Electoral Officer (Canada)"), the following Tuesday or Monday. The governor general may dissolve Parliament and call a general election outside of these fixed dates, conventionally on the advice of the prime minister, which may be preceded by a successful [motion of no confidence](/wiki/Motion_of_no_confidence "Motion of no confidence"). The timing of such dissolutions may be politically motivated.[[28]](#cite_note-29)[[29]](#cite_note-30)[[30]](#cite_note-31)

## Procedure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Parliament_of_Canada&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Procedure")]

Both houses determine [motions](/wiki/Motion_(parliamentary_procedure) "Motion (parliamentary procedure)") by [voice vote](/wiki/Voice_vote "Voice vote"); the presiding officer puts the question and, after listening to shouts of "yea" and "nay" from the members, announces which side is victorious. This decision by the Speaker is final, unless a recorded vote is demanded by members—requiring at least two in the Senate and five in the House of Commons. Members of both houses vote by rising in their places to be counted; the speaker of the Senate is permitted to vote on a motion or bill—though does so irregularly, in the interest of impartiality—and, if there is no majority, the motion is defeated. In the Commons, however, the speaker cannot vote, unless to break a tie. The speaker customarily votes in favour of the [status quo](/wiki/Status_quo "Status quo"). The constitution establishes the [quorums](/wiki/Quorum "Quorum") to be 15 senators in the upper house and 20 members in the lower house, the speaker of each body being counted within the tally.

Voting can thus take three possible forms: whenever possible, leaving the matter open for future consideration and allowing for further discussion by the house; when no further discussion is possible, taking into account that the matter could somehow be brought back in future and be decided by a majority in the house; or, leaving a bill in its existing form rather than having it amended. For example, during the vote on the [2005 budget](/wiki/2005_Canadian_federal_budget "2005 Canadian federal budget"), which was considered a [vote of confidence](/wiki/Motion_of_no_confidence "Motion of no confidence"), the speaker of the House of Commons cast the tie-breaking vote during the [second reading](/wiki/Reading_(legislature)#Second_reading "Reading (legislature)"), moving in favour of the budget and allowing its passage. If the vote on the [third reading](/wiki/Reading_(legislature)#Third_reading "Reading (legislature)") had again been tied, the speaker would have been expected to vote against the bill, bringing down the government.

Simultaneous [interpretation](/wiki/Language_interpretation "Language interpretation") for both official languages, [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") and [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"), is provided at all times during sessions of both houses.

## Legislative functions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Parliament_of_Canada&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Legislative functions")]

Laws, known in their draft form as [bills](/wiki/Bill_(proposed_law) "Bill (proposed law)"), may be introduced by any member of either house. However, most bills originate in the House of Commons, of which most are put forward by [ministers of the Crown](/wiki/Minister_of_the_Crown "Minister of the Crown"), making them government bills, as opposed to [private members' bills](/wiki/Private_members%27_bills "Private members' bills") or private senators' bills, which are launched by MPs and senators, respectively, who are not in cabinet. Draft legislation may also be categorized as public bills, if they apply to the general public, or [private bills](/wiki/Private_bills "Private bills"), if they concern a particular person or limited group of people. Each bill then goes through a series of stages in each chamber, beginning with the [first reading](/wiki/First_reading "First reading"). It is not, however, until the bill's [second reading](/wiki/Second_reading "Second reading") that the general principles of the proposed law are debated; though rejection is a possibility, such is not common for government bills.

Next, the bill is sent by the house where it is being debated to one of several committees. The Standing Orders outline the general mandate for all committees, allowing them to review: bills as they pertain to relevant departments; the program and policy plans, as well as the projected expenditures, and the effectiveness of the implementation thereof, for the same departments; and the analysis of the performance of those departments.[[31]](#cite_note-32) Most often, bills end up before a [standing committee](/wiki/Committee#Standing_committees "Committee"), which is a body of members or senators who specialize in a particular subject (such as [foreign affairs](/wiki/Foreign_affairs "Foreign affairs")), and who may hear testimony from ministers and experts, debate the bill, and recommend amendments. The bill may also be committed to the [Committee of the Whole](/wiki/Committee_of_the_Whole "Committee of the Whole"), a body consisting of, as the name suggests, all the members of the chamber in question. Finally, the bill could be referred to an *ad hoc* committee established solely to review the piece of legislation in question. Each chamber has their own procedure for dealing with this, with the Senate establishing special committees that function like most other committees, and the House of Commons establishing legislative committees, the chair of the latter being appointed by the speaker of the House of Commons, and is normally one of his deputies. Whichever committee is used, any amendments proposed by the committee are considered by the whole house in the report stage. Furthermore, additional amendments not proposed by the committee may also be made.

After the report stage (or, if the committee made no amendments to the bill, immediately after the committee stage), the final phase of the bill—the [third reading](/wiki/Third_reading "Third reading")—occurs, at which time further amendments are not permitted in the House of Commons, but are allowed in the Senate. If it passes the third reading, the bill is sent to the other house of Parliament, where it passes through the same stages;[[note 2]](#cite_note-33) amendments made by the second chamber require the assent of the original house in order to stand part of the final bill. If one house passes amendments that the other will not agree to, and the two houses cannot resolve their disagreements, the bill fails.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/RoyalVisitSenate.jpg/250px-RoyalVisitSenate.jpg)](/wiki/File:RoyalVisitSenate.jpg)

King [George VI](/wiki/George_VI "George VI"), with [Queen Elizabeth](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_The_Queen_Mother "Queen Elizabeth The Queen Mother"), grants [Royal Assent](/wiki/Royal_Assent "Royal Assent") to bills in the Senate chamber, 1939

Once the bill is passed in identical form by both houses, it is presented for [Royal Assent](/wiki/Royal_Assent "Royal Assent"); in theory, the governor general has three options: grant Royal Assent, thereby making the bill into law; withhold Royal Assent, thereby vetoing the bill; or reserve the bill for the signification of the King's [pleasure](/wiki/At_His_Majesty%27s_pleasure "At His Majesty's pleasure"), which allows the sovereign to personally grant or withhold assent. If the governor general does grant Royal Assent, the monarch may, within two years, disallow the bill, thus annulling the law in question. In the federal sphere, no bill has ever been denied royal approval.

In conformity with the British model, only the House of Commons may originate bills for the imposition of taxes or for the appropriation of Crown funds. The constitutional amendment procedure does make provision for the Commons overcoming an otherwise-required Senate resolution in most cases. Otherwise, the theoretical power of both houses over bills is equal, with the assent of each being required for passage. In practice, however, the House of Commons is dominant, with the Senate rarely exercising its powers in a way that opposes the will of the democratically elected house.

## Relationship with the executive

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Parliament_of_Canada&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Relationship with the executive")]

The [federal government](/wiki/Government_of_Canada "Government of Canada") consists of the [monarch (represented by the governor general)-in-council](/wiki/King-in-Council "King-in-Council"), which is a collection of ministers of the Crown appointed by the governor general to direct the use of [executive powers](/wiki/Executive_(government) "Executive (government)"). Per the tenets of [responsible government](/wiki/Responsible_government "Responsible government"), these individuals are almost always drawn from Parliament, and are predominantly from the House of Commons, the only body to which ministers are held accountable, typically during [Question Period](/wiki/Question_Period "Question Period"), wherein ministers are obliged to answer questions posed by members of the opposition. Hence, the person who can command the confidence of the lower chamber—usually the leader of the party with the most seats therein—is typically appointed as prime minister. Should that person not hold a seat in the House of Commons, the prime minister will, by convention, seek election to one at the earliest possible opportunity; frequently, in such situations, a junior member of Parliament who holds a [safe seat](/wiki/Safe_seat "Safe seat") will resign to allow the prime minister to run for that riding in a [by-election](/wiki/By-election "By-election"). If no party holds a majority, it is customary for the governor general to summon a [minority government](/wiki/Minority_governments_in_Canada "Minority governments in Canada") or [coalition government](/wiki/Coalition_government "Coalition government"), depending on which the commons will support.

The lower house may attempt to bring down the government by either rejecting a motion of [confidence](/wiki/Confidence_and_supply "Confidence and supply")—generally initiated by a minister to reinforce the Cabinet's support in the commons—or by passing a motion of no confidence—introduced by the opposition to display its distrust of the Cabinet. Important bills that form part of the government's agenda will usually be considered matters of confidence; the budget is always a matter of confidence. Where a government has lost the confidence of the House of Commons, the prime minister is obliged to either resign (allowing the governor general to appoint the [leader of the Opposition](/wiki/Leader_of_the_Opposition_(Canada) "Leader of the Opposition (Canada)") to the office) or seek the dissolution of Parliament and the call of a general election. A precedent, however, was set in 1968, when the government of [Lester B. Pearson](/wiki/Lester_B._Pearson "Lester B. Pearson") unexpectedly lost a confidence vote but was allowed to remain in power with the mutual consent of the leaders of the other parties.

In practice, the House of Commons' scrutiny of the government is quite weak in comparison to the equivalent chamber in other countries using the [Westminster system](/wiki/Westminster_system "Westminster system"). With the [plurality voting system](/wiki/Plurality_voting_system "Plurality voting system") used in parliamentary elections tending to provide the governing party with a large majority, and a party system that gives leaders strict control over their caucus (to the point that MPs may be expelled from their parties for voting against the instructions of party leaders), there is often limited need to compromise with other parties. Additionally, Canada has fewer MPs, a higher turnover rate of MPs after each election, and an Americanized system for selecting political party leaders, leaving them accountable to the party membership rather than caucus, as is the case in the United Kingdom;[[32]](#cite_note-34) John Robson of the *National Post* opined that Canada's parliament had become a body akin to the [American Electoral College](/wiki/Electoral_College_(United_States) "Electoral College (United States)"), "its sole and ceremonial role to confirm the executive in power."[[33]](#cite_note-35)

At the end of the 20th century and into the 21st, analysts—such as [Jeffrey Simpson](/wiki/Jeffrey_Simpson "Jeffrey Simpson"), [Donald Savoie](/wiki/Donald_Savoie "Donald Savoie"), and [John Gomery](/wiki/John_Gomery "John Gomery")—argued that both Parliament and the Cabinet had become eclipsed by prime ministerial power.[[34]](#cite_note-36) Thus, defeats of majority governments on issues of confidence are very rare. In contrast, a minority government is more volatile, and is more likely to fall due to loss of confidence. The last prime ministers to lose confidence votes were [Stephen Harper](/wiki/Stephen_Harper "Stephen Harper") in 2011, [Paul Martin](/wiki/Paul_Martin "Paul Martin") in 2005 and [Joe Clark](/wiki/Joe_Clark "Joe Clark") in 1979, all involving minority governments. The passage of the *[Reform Act](/wiki/Reform_Act_(Canada) "Reform Act (Canada)")* and resulting changes to the *[Parliament of Canada Act](/wiki/Parliament_of_Canada_Act "Parliament of Canada Act")*, in 2015, were a response to this trend and an attempt to increase the power and independence of MPs.[[35]](#cite_note-:1-37)[[36]](#cite_note-38)[[37]](#cite_note-39)[[38]](#cite_note-40)

## Privileges

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Parliament_of_Canada&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Privileges")]

Parliament possesses a number of privileges, collectively and accordingly known as [parliamentary privilege](/wiki/Parliamentary_privilege "Parliamentary privilege"), each house being the guardian and administrator of its own set of rights. Parliament itself determines the extent of parliamentary privilege, each house overseeing its own affairs, but the constitution bars it from conferring any "exceeding those at the passing of such an Act held, enjoyed, and exercised by the [British House of] Commons... and by the Members thereof."[[39]](#cite_note-41)

The foremost dispensation held by both houses of Parliament is that of [freedom of speech](/wiki/Freedom_of_speech "Freedom of speech") in debate; nothing said within the chambers may be questioned by any court or other institution outside of Parliament. In particular, a member of either house cannot be sued for [slander](/wiki/Slander_and_libel "Slander and libel") based on words uttered in the course of parliamentary proceedings, the only restraint on debate being set by the standing orders of each house. Further, MPs and senators are immune to arrest in civil (but not criminal) cases, from jury service and attendance in courts as witnesses. They may, however, be disciplined by their colleagues for breach of the rules, including [contempt of Parliament](/wiki/Contempt_of_parliament "Contempt of parliament")—disobedience of its authority; for example, giving false testimony before a parliamentary committee—and breaches of its own privileges.

The [Canadian Heraldic Authority](/wiki/Canadian_Heraldic_Authority "Canadian Heraldic Authority"), on 15 April 2008, confirmed the right of the Parliament of Canada to use a [heraldic badge](/wiki/Heraldic_badge "Heraldic badge") composed of symbols of the three elements of Parliament: the [escutcheon](/wiki/Escutcheon_(heraldry) "Escutcheon (heraldry)") of the [Royal Arms of Canada](/wiki/Arms_of_Canada "Arms of Canada") with the maces of the House of Commons and Senate crossed behind.[[40]](#cite_note-42)

The budget for the Parliament of Canada for the 2010 [fiscal year](/wiki/Fiscal_year "Fiscal year") was [$](/wiki/Canadian_dollar "Canadian dollar")583,567,000.[[41]](#cite_note-43)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Parliament_of_Canada&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: History")]

Following the cession of [New France](/wiki/New_France "New France") to the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") in the [1763 Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1763) "Treaty of Paris (1763)"), Canada was governed according to the [Royal Proclamation](/wiki/Royal_Proclamation_of_1763 "Royal Proclamation of 1763") issued by [King George III](/wiki/George_III_of_the_United_Kingdom "George III of the United Kingdom") in that same year. To this was added the [Quebec Act](/wiki/Quebec_Act "Quebec Act"), by which the power to make ordinances was granted to a [governor-in-council](/wiki/Queen-in-Council "Queen-in-Council"), both the governor and council being appointed by the British monarch in Westminster, on the advice of his or her ministers there. In 1791, the Province of Quebec was divided into [Upper](/wiki/Upper_Canada "Upper Canada") and [Lower Canada](/wiki/Lower_Canada "Lower Canada"), each with an elected [legislative assembly](/wiki/Legislative_assembly "Legislative assembly"), an appointed [legislative council](/wiki/Legislative_council "Legislative council"), and a governor, mirroring the parliamentary structure in Britain.

During the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812"), [American troops](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army") [set fire](/wiki/Battle_of_York#Burning_of_York "Battle of York") to the [buildings](/wiki/Ontario_Legislative_Building#History "Ontario Legislative Building") of the [Legislative Assembly of Upper Canada](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_Upper_Canada "Legislative Assembly of Upper Canada") in [York](/wiki/York,_Upper_Canada "York, Upper Canada") (now [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto")). In 1841, the British government united the two Canadas into the [Province of Canada](/wiki/Province_of_Canada "Province of Canada"), with a single [legislature](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_Province_of_Canada "Parliament of the Province of Canada") composed of, again, an assembly, council, and governor general; the 84 members of the lower chamber were equally divided among the two former provinces, though Lower Canada had a higher population. The governor still held significant personal influence over Canadian affairs until 1848, when [responsible government](/wiki/Responsible_government "Responsible government") was implemented in Canada.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Incendie_Parlement_Montreal.jpg/250px-Incendie_Parlement_Montreal.jpg)](/wiki/File:Incendie_Parlement_Montreal.jpg)

The burning of the Parliament in [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal"), 1849

The actual site of Parliament shifted on a regular basis: From 1841 to 1844, it sat in [Kingston](/wiki/Kingston,_Ontario "Kingston, Ontario"), where the present [Kingston General Hospital](/wiki/Kingston_General_Hospital "Kingston General Hospital") now stands; from 1844 until the [1849 fire that destroyed the building](/wiki/Burning_of_the_Parliament_Buildings_in_Montreal "Burning of the Parliament Buildings in Montreal"), the legislature was in [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal"); and, after a few years of alternating between Toronto and [Quebec City](/wiki/Quebec_City "Quebec City"), the legislature was finally moved to [Ottawa](/wiki/Ottawa "Ottawa") in 1856, [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria") having chosen that city as Canada's capital in 1857.[[42]](#cite_note-44)

The modern-day Parliament of Canada came into existence in 1867, in which year the Parliament of the [United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland") passed the [*British North America Act, 1867*](/wiki/Constitution_Act,_1867 "Constitution Act, 1867"), uniting the provinces of [New Brunswick](/wiki/New_Brunswick "New Brunswick"), [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia"), and Canada—with the Province of Canada split into [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec") and [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario")—into a single federation known as [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"). Though the form of the new federal legislature was again nearly identical to the Parliament of the United Kingdom, the decision to retain this model was made with heavy influence from the just-concluded [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), which indicated to many [Canadians](/wiki/Canadians "Canadians") the faults of the [American](/wiki/United_States "United States") federal system, with its relatively powerful states and a less powerful federal government. The *British North America Act* limited the powers of the provinces, providing that all subjects not explicitly delegated to them by that document remain within the authority of the Canadian Parliament, while simultaneously giving the provinces unique powers in certain agreed-upon areas of jurisdiction.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Opening_session_of_the_House_of_Commons_at_the_Victoria_Memorial_Museum_after_the_Parliament_Buildings_fire_of_1916.jpg/250px-Opening_session_of_the_House_of_Commons_at_the_Victoria_Memorial_Museum_after_the_Parliament_Buildings_fire_of_1916.jpg)](/wiki/File:Opening_session_of_the_House_of_Commons_at_the_Victoria_Memorial_Museum_after_the_Parliament_Buildings_fire_of_1916.jpg)

The first session of the House of Commons in its temporary location at the [Victoria Memorial Museum](/wiki/Canadian_Museum_of_Nature "Canadian Museum of Nature"), 18 March 1918

Full legislative autonomy was granted by the [Statute of Westminster, 1931](/wiki/Statute_of_Westminster_1931 "Statute of Westminster 1931"), passed by the Parliament of the United Kingdom. Though the statute allowed the Parliament of Canada to repeal or amend previously British laws as they applied to Canada, it did not permit amendment to Canada's constitution, including the British North America Acts. Hence, whenever a constitutional amendment was sought by the Canadian Parliament, the enactment of a British law was necessary, though Canada's consent was required. The Parliament of Canada was granted limited power to amend the constitution by a British Act of Parliament in 1949, but it was not permitted to affect the powers of provincial governments, the official positions of the English and French languages, rights of any class of persons with respect to schools, or the maximum five-year term of the legislature.

While her father, King [George VI](/wiki/George_VI "George VI"), had been the first Canadian monarch to grant royal assent in the legislature—doing so in 1939—Queen [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") was the first sovereign to deliver the [speech from the throne](/wiki/Speech_from_the_throne "Speech from the throne"). This event, in 1957, was the first time television cameras were allowed into the chambers of parliament, as the [Canadian Broadcasting Corporation](/wiki/Canadian_Broadcasting_Corporation "Canadian Broadcasting Corporation") broadcast the speech nation-wide.[[43]](#cite_note-45)

The Canadian House of Commons and Senate last requested the Parliament of the United Kingdom to enact a constitutional amendment in 1982, in the form of the *[Canada Act 1982](/wiki/Canada_Act_1982 "Canada Act 1982")* which included the *[Constitution Act, 1982](/wiki/Constitution_Act,_1982 "Constitution Act, 1982")*.[[44]](#cite_note-46) This legislation terminated the power of the British Parliament's ability to legislate for Canada and the authority to amend the constitution was transferred to the Canadian House of Commons, the Senate, and the provincial legislative assemblies, acting jointly. Most amendments require the consent of the Senate, the House of Commons, and the [legislative assemblies](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly "Legislative Assembly") of two-thirds of the provinces representing a majority of the population; the unanimous consent of provincial legislative assemblies is required for certain amendments, including those affecting the sovereign, the governor general, the [provincial lieutenant governors](/wiki/Lieutenant_Governor_(Canada) "Lieutenant Governor (Canada)"), the official status of the English and French languages, the [Supreme Court of Canada](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Canada "Supreme Court of Canada"), and the amending formulas themselves.
